{{Short description|Physical quantity that expresses hot and cold}}
{{Infobox physics
| name = Temperature
| image = Thermometer CF.svg
| caption = A [[thermometer]] calibrated in degrees [[Celsius]] (left) and [[Fahrenheit]] (right).
| topics = [[Laws of thermodynamics]], [[Entropy]], [[Kinetic theory of gases]], [[Statistical mechanics]]
| SI_unit = [[kelvin]] (K)
| other_units = [[Celsius]] (°C), [[Fahrenheit]] (°F)
}}

'''Temperature''' is a physical quantity that expresses the subjective perceptions of hot and cold. Scientifically, it is proportional to the average [[kinetic energy]] of the random microscopic motions of the constituent particles of a system. Temperature is a fundamental parameter in [[thermodynamics]] and [[statistical mechanics]].

Temperature is measured with a [[thermometer]], which may be calibrated in various [[temperature scale]]s. The most common scales are the [[Celsius]] scale (denoted °C), the [[Fahrenheit]] scale (°F), and the [[Kelvin]] scale (K), with Kelvin being the [[SI base unit]].

== Thermodynamic definition ==
{{Main|Thermodynamic temperature}}
From the [[second law of thermodynamics]], temperature is defined via the efficiency of an ideal [[Carnot heat engine]]:
:<math>\frac{T_C}{T_H} = 1 - \eta</math>
where <math>\eta</math> is the maximum possible efficiency between temperatures <math>T_C</math> (cold) and <math>T_H</math> (hot).

=== Absolute zero ===
The lowest theoretical temperature is [[absolute zero]] (0 K or −273.15°C), at which the particles of a substance have minimal kinetic energy. The [[third law of thermodynamics]] states that absolute zero cannot be reached through any finite number of processes.

== Kinetic theory ==
{{Main|Kinetic theory of gases}}
In the kinetic theory of [[ideal gas|gases]], temperature is directly proportional to the average translational kinetic energy of molecules:
:<math>\frac{1}{2}m\langle v^2 \rangle = \frac{3}{2}k_B T</math>
where <math>k_B</math> is the [[Boltzmann constant]] and <math>m</math> is the molecular mass.

== Statistical mechanics definition ==
{{Main|Statistical mechanics}}
From statistical mechanics, temperature is related to the rate of change of [[entropy]] <math>S</math> with respect to [[internal energy]] <math>U</math>:
:<math>\frac{1}{T} = \left(\frac{\partial S}{\partial U}\right)_{V, N}</math>

This definition gives temperature as the reciprocal of the derivative of entropy with respect to energy, linking macroscopic thermodynamics to microscopic statistical behavior.

== Temperature scales ==
{| class="wikitable"
|-
! Scale !! Freezing water !! Boiling water
|-
| Kelvin || 273.15 K || 373.15 K
|-
| Celsius || 0°C || 100°C
|-
| Fahrenheit || 32°F || 212°F
|}

== Effects of temperature ==
* [[Thermal expansion]] — most materials expand when heated
* Change of [[electrical resistivity|electrical resistance]] — typically increases with temperature in metals
* Phase transitions — [[melting]], [[boiling]], [[sublimation]]
* Speed of [[sound]] — increases with temperature in gases
* [[Black-body radiation]] — spectrum shifts with temperature ([[Wien's displacement law]])

== See also ==
{{Portal|Physics}}
* [[Entropy]]
* [[Heat]]
* [[Ideal gas law]]
* [[Kinetic theory of gases]]
* [[Laws of thermodynamics]]
* [[Statistical mechanics]]
* [[Thermodynamics]]

== References ==
{{Reflist|30em}}

[[Category:Thermodynamics]]
[[Category:Physical quantities]]
[[Category:Heat transfer]]
